question: How many Grand Slam singles titles did Serena Williams win in her career?
funFact: Serena Williams won her 23rd Grand Slam title at the 2017 Australian Open while in the first trimester of pregnancy.

question: Wimbledon uses which playing surface?
funFact: Wimbledon's courts use 100% perennial ryegrass, cut to precisely 8mm. The courts are reseeded every year after the championships.

question: What score is referred to as "deuce" in tennis?
funFact: The word "deuce" likely comes from the French "à deux le jeu" (to both the game), though the exact etymology is disputed.

question: Roger Federer won a record-equalling 20 Grand Slam singles titles before Djokovic and Nadal surpassed him. At which tournament did he win his first?
funFact: Federer spent a record 310 weeks as world number one over his career, a record later broken by Novak Djokovic.

question: Which annual tennis tournament is played on red clay in Paris?
funFact: Roland Garros is named after a French aviator who was the first person to fly across the Mediterranean Sea.

question: In what year did open tennis (allowing professionals alongside amateurs) begin?
funFact: Before 1968, professionals were banned from Grand Slams. The "Open Era" transformed tennis into a fully professional sport.

question: Who founded the Women's Tennis Association (WTA) in June 1973?
funFact: Billie Jean King founded the WTA alongside eight other players known as the "Original 9", who signed $1 contracts to launch the first Virginia Slims circuit.

question: How many weeks was Steffi Graf ranked world No. 1, a record that still stands?
funFact: Graf's 377 weeks at No. 1 surpasses all other players in tennis history, male or female. She finished year-end No. 1 a record eight times.

question: Serena Williams won her first Grand Slam singles title at which tournament?
funFact: Serena Williams turned professional in 1995, and claimed her breakthrough Grand Slam title at the 1999 US Open aged just 17, defeating Martina Hingis in the final.

question: Martina Navratilova holds the all-time record for women's Wimbledon singles titles. How many did she win?
funFact: Navratilova reached the Wimbledon singles final 12 times in total, winning nine of them. She won a run of six consecutive titles from 1982 to 1987.

question: Steffi Graf is the only tennis player — male or female — to have achieved the "Golden Slam". What does this mean?
correctAnswers: Winning all four Grand Slams and the Olympic gold medal in the same calendar year
wrongAnswers: Winning all four Grand Slams in a single calendar year
Winning 20 or more Grand Slam titles
Winning each Grand Slam at least once and reaching world No. 1
Winning a Grand Slam and the WTA Finals in the same year
funFact: Graf achieved the Golden Slam in 1988, winning the Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon, US Open, and the Olympic gold medal at Seoul — all in the same year.

question: The inaugural Virginia Slims tournament in 1970 — which paved the way for the WTA — was won by which player?
funFact: The Virginia Slims tournament, held on 23 September 1970 at the Houston Racquet Club, was the first major step toward an independent women's professional circuit.

question: Serena Williams is the only player to accomplish a career Golden Slam in both singles and doubles. How many Grand Slam singles titles did she win in the Open Era, the most of any player?
funFact: Serena's 23 Open Era Grand Slam singles titles surpass Steffi Graf's 22. Her 2017 Australian Open title is especially remarkable as she was pregnant at the time.

question: How many weeks was Martina Navratilova ranked world No. 1 in women's singles?
funFact: Navratilova's 332 weeks at world No. 1 is second only to Steffi Graf's record 377 weeks. She was year-end No. 1 seven times across her career.

question: Where are the WTA's corporate headquarters located?
funFact: The WTA has its corporate headquarters in St. Petersburg, Florida, with European offices in London and Asia-Pacific offices in Beijing.

question: What was the name of Serena Williams's dominant stretch from the 2002 French Open to the 2003 Australian Open, when she held all four Grand Slam titles simultaneously?
funFact: During the "Serena Slam", Serena won four consecutive Grand Slams — each time defeating her sister Venus Williams in the final.

question: Steffi Graf won how many Grand Slam singles titles in her career, the second-most in the Open Era?
funFact: Graf's 22 Grand Slam singles titles include at least four titles at each of the four majors — a feat no other player, male or female, has achieved.

question: Martina Navratilova recorded an extraordinary win percentage in 1983. What was her record for the season?
funFact: Navratilova's 1983 season win percentage of 98.9% (86–1) is the best professional season winning percentage in tennis history.

question: Women's tennis made its Olympic debut at which Games?
funFact: Women's tennis was first included at the 1900 Paris Olympics, though women's doubles was not added until the 1920 Antwerp Games.

question: Serena Williams was ranked world No. 1 for 319 weeks during her career. Where does this rank all-time in women's tennis?
funFact: Steffi Graf leads with 377 weeks at No. 1, Navratilova is second with 332 weeks, and Serena Williams is third with 319 weeks — all three are Open Era legends.

question: Who was Steffi Graf's iconic on-court rival whose dominance she ended to become world No. 1?
funFact: Graf won her first major at the 1987 French Open, signalling the end of the Navratilova–Evert era and the start of her own decade-long dominance of women's tennis.

question: How many Open Era Grand Slam titles did Martina Navratilova win in total (singles, doubles, and mixed doubles)?
funFact: Navratilova's 49 Open Era major titles break down as 18 singles, a record 31 women's doubles, and a record 10 mixed doubles titles.

question: At what age did Steffi Graf become the youngest player to appear in the main draw of a Grand Slam?
funFact: Graf made her Grand Slam debut at the 1983 French Open aged just 13, becoming the youngest player ever to appear in a major main draw at that time.

question: One of the earliest superstars of women's tennis launched the first professional women's tour in North America in 1926–27. Who was she?
funFact: Suzanne Lenglen won six Wimbledon titles and then turned professional to lead the first women's pro tour, revolutionising women's tennis and making it a spectator sport.

question: Serena Williams won the Laureus World Sports Award for Sportswoman of the Year a record four times. Which years did she win it?
funFact: Serena's four Laureus Sportswoman of the Year awards are more than any other athlete in the award's history, reflecting her sustained dominance over two decades.

question: Which Grand Slam did Steffi Graf win first, launching her era of dominance?
funFact: Graf's first Grand Slam title came at Roland Garros in 1987, defeating Navratilova. The following year she swept all four majors and the Olympics for the Golden Slam.

question: How many WTA singles titles did Martina Navratilova win across her career — an Open Era record?
funFact: Navratilova's 167 singles titles and 177 doubles titles are both Open Era records. Her serve-and-volley style was revolutionary and set the standard for athletic fitness in tennis.

question: Which country's national championship did Martina Navratilova win in 1972, at just 15 years old?
funFact: Born in Prague, Navratilova won the Czechoslovak national championship aged 15 before defecting to the United States in 1975 to pursue her professional career.

question: Serena Williams's sister Venus also competed at the highest level. What did Serena and Venus share in common during the 2002–2003 "Serena Slam"?
correctAnswers: Venus was the runner-up in all four Grand Slam finals that Serena won
wrongAnswers: They won women's doubles together at all four Grand Slams
They reached the semi-finals together at each Grand Slam
They were both ranked No. 1 simultaneously
Venus won the mixed doubles at the same Grand Slams
funFact: During Serena's dominant 2002–2003 Slam run, every one of those four finals was an all-Williams affair, with Venus finishing as runner-up each time.

question: How many WTA singles titles did Steffi Graf win across her career?
funFact: Graf's 107 singles titles include 22 Grand Slam titles and are the third-highest total in the Open Era behind Navratilova's 167 and Evert's 157.

question: Navratilova is famous for her serve-and-volley playing style. Which coach worked with her from 1981 to 1983 and helped refine her tactics?
funFact: Renée Richards, a pioneering transgender woman and former tennis professional, coached Navratilova and is credited with sharpening the tactical awareness that helped Martina dominate the early 1980s.

question: Which nickname was given to Steffi Graf based on her signature shot?
funFact: Graf's devastating inside-out forehand earned her the nickname "Fräulein Forehand". Her unique swing generated exceptional pace and won countless crucial points.

question: The WTA was founded with nine founding players known as the "Original 9". Which of the following was NOT one of them?
funFact: The Original 9 — including Billie Jean King, Rosie Casals, and Nancy Richey — each signed $1 contracts to launch the WTA circuit. Chris Evert, though a contemporary, was not among the original nine.

question: Serena Williams was born on September 26, 1981, in which city?
funFact: Although Serena was born in Saginaw, Michigan, her family moved to Compton, California, where she and Venus honed their tennis on public courts under the coaching of their father Richard.

question: Martina Navratilova holds the women's record for the longest all-surface winning streak in professional tennis. How many consecutive match wins did she record?
funFact: Navratilova's 74-match all-surface winning streak is a professional record for women. She also won six consecutive Grand Slam singles titles across 1983 and 1984.

question: Which women's tennis player is widely considered the first superstar of the sport, winning six Wimbledon titles before turning professional in the 1920s?
funFact: French player Suzanne Lenglen dominated the 1920s and drew massive crowds to Wimbledon. Her professional tour of North America in 1926–27 was the first women's pro tennis tour in history.
